Khatsahlano Street Party returns after two-year hiatus   The Khatsahlano Street Festival, after a two-year COVID hiatus, was in full swing on Saturday, headlined by the punk/new wave band Pointed Sticks

Province dismisses seven members of B.C. Housing board after external review   Effective immediately, the board is solely comprised of Allan Seckel (chair) and recently appointed board members Jill Kot, Sheila Taylor, Mark Sieben and Russ Jones.
B.C. dad and son honoured for rescuing two men in a capsized canoe   RCMP Cpl. James Moore and his 11-year-old son Emmett saved the two men from drowning in Griffin Lake near Revelstoke last summer.

COVID-19 update for July 8-10: B.C. will roll out fall booster shot program for people age 12+ | Dix says province in new Omicron wave | VCH employee alleges discrimination over lack of precautions at work   Here's your daily update with everything you need to know on the coronavirus situation in B.C. and around the world.

Iconic bronze sculpture 'The Family' unveiled in new permanent home on Granville Island   The sculpture, which was originally in front of the old Pacific Press (Vancouver Sun) building at 7th and Granville Street in the '60s, sat in storage for nearly 15 years.

Proposed 2030 Winter Olympics could cost up to $4 billion   The cost would require $1 billion–$1.2 billion in public funds to cover costs to refurbish venues, security and contribute to the construction of athletes villages.

Affidavits for Shangri-La tower class-action lawsuit examine market values   The case began in December 2015 when the lead plaintiff filed a notice of civil claim, alleging that defective glass windows were fogging, leaking and spontaneously breaking.
B.C. adds 6,100 jobs in June, while employment down overall in Canada   B.C.'s unemployment rate edged up to 4.6 per cent last month from 4.5 per cent in May.

True Crime Byline: How one childhood incident pushed Dawn Crey into a downward spiral from which she would never escape   The new podcast True Crime Byline looks back at The Vancouver Sun investigation into Vancouver's missing women, a case that eventually led to the arrest and conviction of Robert Pickton. This profile of missing woman Dawn Crey was part of a series of investigative stories published in 2001.

True Crime Byline: A Politician Murdered his Ex Wife. We Should Have Seen it Coming   Looking back on the murder of JoAnn Wilson today, what's most surprising is that we were surprised it happened at all.

True Crime Byline podcast: The Article that Blew Up a Murder Trial   Listen to this podcast episode for the shocking story of how a National Post article about a teen vampire led to a mistrial, as told by reporter Joseph Brean

Douglas Todd: Canadian cops far less likely to kill, or be killed, than U.S. counterparts   Analysis: Canadian police are often accused of using excessive force, but a U.S. officer is five times more likely than a Canadian cop to shoot and kill.
Vaughn Palmer: Showy B.C. NDP incentive offer to young doctors a big dud   Opinion: One doctor called the Health Ministry's offer a 'randomly thought-up, Band-Aid solution.' So far, no takers.

Dan Fumano: Time for Vancouver to rethink how housing is approved?   Opinion: Vancouver city council has run out of time this term for a major housing decision. Will the next council consider changes to public hearing requirements?

Lions set to test their mettle against Bombers in battle of undefeated teams   After beating B.C. by a combined score of 75-9 in a pair of blowout wins last season, Winnipeg will see a team that has been firing on all cylinders on the way to its first 3-0 start since 2007.

Darling dresses: 10 summer styles to step out in this season   As an ode to this summertime staple, we've rounded up 10 favourites.

Makeover: Daughter's wedding was a great incentive to fix nurse's neglected hair   Josephina Gowler is a 50-year-old nurse who is required to wear her hair up for work. She has neglected her hair for several months and with her daughter’s wedding fast approaching, a new look is exactly what the doctor ordered.

Lucinda Turner, who worked to combat illicit trade in Indigenous art, has died at 63   Turner, an accomplished artist who had apprenticed and collaborated with Nisg'aa sculptor Norman Tait, died on Monday.

Brian Minter: It's still not too late for your food gardens   Here we are, in early July — a time when many folks think the vegetable gardening season is over, when actually, it’s the perfect time to begin a food garden and to have it thrive into the winter months.
The Home Front: Motivated to do good work   Vancouver architects Tracey Mactavish and Asher deGroot, of Motiv Architects, aim to build community connections

Condo Smarts: Tony Gioventu pens 1,000th column on strata life   Weekly Homes columnist began helping readers with strata-living issues in November, 2002
Two separate plants both called 'sun rose' and 'rock rose'   Both are Mediterranean plants with a preference for similar growing conditions: a very well-drained soil, and full sun.

Home sales plummet in Metro Vancouver as buyers adjust to interest rate hikes   Prospective buyers are now being forced to weigh the positive impact of falling prices against the negative impact of rising interest rates

Collector Classics: 1962 Chevrolet Corvette convertible   It was a leap of faith that couples sometimes need to make. He saw a big withdrawal from their joint savings account. She would only say it was a good investment and he needed to trust her judgment. She added that their financial advisor was in favour of the expense.
2023 Toyota Crown teaser hints at sedan-style SUV   The patent images being passed around now seem legit, with the new SUV to be properly revealed on July 15

'ALS will be a treatable chronic disease': New treatment slows disease progression and increases life expectancy   McGill University's Dr. Angela Genge is optimistic about what the future holds for amyotrophic lateral sclerosis care: "Every step gives our patients more time, better function for longer, better care every year."

Our friends may be our friends because of the way they smell   When it comes to judging other people on their body odour, we may be more like other mammals than we realize, according to a new study.

Putting our phones on silent mode only makes them more tempting, study says   Blame FOMO for how hard it can be for us to stop looking at our phones all the time.
Ontario police seize cannabis worth $61 million   Estimated 45,000-plus illegal pot plants and eight metric tonnes of processed weed confiscated.

Joe Rogan responds to border drug seizure: ‘You should take all these drugs and rethink your life’   Border agents seized small amounts of ecstasy, cannabis and psilocybin mushrooms from a vehicle entering New York.
One of Canada's biggest universities will have a pot store on campus   The planned store would represent the pot company Burb's eighth and final one allowed in the province under its retail cap.

Man's ploy to flush over 20 kilograms of cannabis down the toilet leads him straight to jail   Officers also found 21 kilograms of weed, additional bags of prepackaged marijuana, a handgun and over $16K in cash.
